title: Every Call to Your Shop That Goes to Voicemail Is an Invoice You're Torching | Booked Job
h1: Every Call That Goes to Voicemail Is an Invoice You're Torching
meta: A big chunk of calls to home-services shops go to voicemail. Here's the blunt math on the invoices that turns into — and why answering beats buying more leads.
short: A meaningful share of calls to your shop go straight to voicemail. Each one is a potential job walking to the next guy. Answer the phone before you buy another lead.
statLabel: Every call that rings out to voicemail is a missed invoice, not a missed "lead."

body.answer: A real chunk of the calls to your shop go straight to voicemail.
body.html: A real chunk of the calls to your shop go straight to voicemail. On the phone channel, a missed call isn't a soft "lead" you can nurture later. It's someone with a broken water heater or a dead AC dialing down a list. If you don't pick up, the next name on Google does. Stop calling it a missed lead. It's a missed invoice.

body.h2: Do the blunt math on your own line
body.answer: Take your own numbers — you know them, I don't, so I'm not going to make any up.
body.html:
Take your own numbers — you know them, I don't, so I'm not going to make any up. Count how many calls hit your line last month. Figure out how many rang out to voicemail.

Now attach a ticket to each one. Use your own average job value — you know what a plumbing or HVAC job is worth to you better than any benchmark does. Not every missed call would've booked — but not every one is junk, either. Say even a fraction of them were real jobs. Multiply that by your ticket and you're looking at real money in work you never quoted, because a phone rang and nobody answered.

Run it on your actual call count and your actual ticket. The number usually stops the room.

body.h2: Why a missed call is worse than a missed ad click
body.html:
Here's the part that stings. The person calling you already found you, already trusted you enough to dial, and already has a problem they'll pay to fix today. That's the warmest lead in the entire funnel — and it's the one you're dropping.

And they don't wait. Most homeowners hire whoever responds first, and responding fast beats responding late by a wide margin. Voicemail isn't a short delay. It's a "call the next guy" delay.

body.h2: You don't have a lead problem. You have an answering problem.
body.answer: Most contractors react to a slow month by buying more leads.
body.html:
Most contractors react to a slow month by buying more leads. But if calls are already dying at voicemail, you're pouring new water into a bucket with a hole in it. Every lead source you buy — Google LSA, Angi, referrals — still ends in a phone call. Miss the call and you paid for a lead you never worked.

Worse, the expensive lead networks assume you answer. Miss the calls those generate and your real cost per booked job climbs even higher, because you're eating the lead cost with nothing to show.

body.h2: Fix the leak before you buy a drop
body.html:
Coverage beats volume. Before you spend another dollar on leads, plug the hole:

1. Answer live during business hours. A human, every call. This is free and it's the highest-paying change on this list.

2. Have overflow coverage. When you're under a sink or on a roof, route to a second line, an answering service, or a booking assistant — not voicemail.

3. Text back every missed call in minutes. If a call slips through, an automatic "Sorry we missed you — what do you need?" text catches a chunk of those callers before they dial your competitor.

4. Track it. Watch your missed-call rate the same way you watch your bank balance. If it's creeping up, that's payroll on the floor.

body.answer: Calls going to voicemail isn't a phone problem — it's a revenue problem wearing a phone costume.
body.html: Calls going to voicemail isn't a phone problem — it's a revenue problem wearing a phone costume. Each of those calls is a homeowner with a job worth real money, and most of them hire whoever picks up first. Answer the phone. It's cheaper than any lead you'll ever buy.

faq.q: What's a normal missed-call rate for a contractor?
faq.a: Any missed call is a job your marketing already paid to generate walking out the door. The goal is to get as close to zero as your coverage allows — track your own rate and drive it down.

faq.q: How much does a missed call actually cost me?
faq.a: Depends on your ticket. Count your monthly calls, figure out how many hit voicemail, and assume even a fraction would've booked — the leaked revenue is almost always bigger than owners guess.

faq.q: Should I buy more leads or fix my phone coverage first?
faq.a: Fix coverage first. Every lead source ends in a call, and if calls are already dying at voicemail, buying more leads just feeds the leak. Missing the calls your paid leads generate makes every one of them more expensive.

faq.q: Does a text-back on missed calls really help?
faq.a: Yes. Most homeowners hire the first contractor to respond, and a fast reply beats a slow one, so an automatic text back the instant a call is missed catches people before they dial the next name on Google.

threads.text: Every call to your shop that goes straight to voicemail isn't a missed 'lead' — it's a missed invoice. It's a homeowner with a job they'll pay to fix today, dialing down a list until someone answers. Answer the phone before you buy another lead.

bluesky.text: A real chunk of calls to home-services shops go unanswered. Each one's a homeowner with a job they'll pay for today, dialing down a list. Most hire whoever answers first. Fix the phone before you buy more leads.

mastodon.text: Blunt math for contractors: calls that hit voicemail aren't missed leads. They're missed invoices — homeowners with a job they'll pay to fix today. Most hire the first to respond. Answering beats buying leads.

telegram.text: Every call to your shop that goes to voicemail is a job calling the next guy. Homeowners hire whoever answers first. Stop buying leads. Answer the phone.

fbPosts: Contractors: every call to your shop that goes straight to voicemail isn't a missed 'lead' you can chase later. It's a homeowner with a job they'll pay to fix today, dialing down a list until someone answers. Most of them hire whoever picks up first. Count your calls, figure out how many hit voicemail, attach a ticket to each — the number will stop you cold. Answer the phone before you spend another dollar on leads.

You don't have a lead problem. You have an answering problem. A real chunk of calls to home-services shops go unanswered. Every lead you buy still ends in a phone call, so if it's ringing out to voicemail you're pouring money into a bucket with a hole in it. The fix is free: answer live, cover the overflow, and text back every missed call in minutes. Cheaper than any lead network. Plug the leak first.

unsourcedClaims: 78% hire the first contractor to respond (Lead Connect 2026)
100x more likely to qualify within 5 min vs 30 (MIT Sloan 2026)
'five jobs times $1,714 — north of eight grand' example math
_verdict.notes: The allowed sourced-stats list is undefined/empty, so ZERO numeric claims are permitted. Every figure in the original (14%, 1 in 7, $1,714, $2,110, 78%, 100x, $542, $168, and all example math like '20 calls' and 'eight grand') is unsourced and was removed or replaced with qualitative language. statBig cannot be a number under these rules, so it was set to a qualitative headline phrase and statLabel de-numbered. Tone is on-brand: blunt, direct, no corporate filler — kept intact. allNumbersSourced=false because no numbers survive as sourced.
